item.title: Taveuni could run short of food as shipping disruption continues
item.description: A Taveuni supermarket owner is warning that the island could soon face shortages of food, fuel and other essential supplies unless shipping services resume soon. Speaking to fijivillage News, Sun City Supermarket owner Rohil Chaudhari says the disruption has already affected businesses and residents across the island.
item.content:encoded:
A Taveuni supermarket owner is warning that the island could soon face shortages of food, fuel and other essential supplies unless shipping services resume soon.

Speaking to fijivillage News, Sun City Supermarket owner Rohil Chaudhari says the disruption has already affected businesses and residents across the island.

He says farmers are unable to transport and sell produce such as yaqona and dalo, leaving many without an income.

He says his truck has been stranded in Suva since last week after being fully loaded with goods for Taveuni, and remains parked inside a vessel because services are still suspended.

Chaudhari says while discussions continue, it is the people of Taveuni who are paying the price, with businesses running low on stock, fuel becoming scarce and families worried about essential supplies.

He says his driver and staff have also been away from their families while waiting for a solution so the cargo can be delivered to the island.

Chaudhari is calling on the Government and relevant authorities to work together and restore shipping services as soon as possible.

Chaudhari says they knew shipping companies had suspended services but did not expect the disruption to last this long.

He is also calling on the Maritime Safety Authority of Fiji to explain why shipping services remain suspended and what is being done to restore them.

Chaudhari is urging the Government, MSAF, shipping companies and all parties involved to work together to find an urgent solution.

He says if the current vessels cannot operate, alternative arrangements should be made so essential supplies can continue reaching the island.

He stresses that the issue is not about taking sides, but about ensuring the people of Taveuni continue to have access to food, fuel, medicine and other basic necessities.

Chaudhari hopes Taveuni is not forgotten as efforts continue to resolve the shipping disruption.

We have contacted the Minister for Transport, Ro Filipe Tuisawau and Minister for Immigration, Viliame Naupoto. They are yet to comment.

item.title: Commission hears calls for greater powers for Permanent Secretaries and mandatory investment of resource income in USP submission
item.description: Greater decision-making powers should be returned to Permanent Secretaries instead of being concentrated in the hands of Ministers. USP linguistics lecturer Rajendra Prasad made the submission at the University of the South Pacific, saying the 2013 Constitution gives Ministers too much authority, limiting the ability of Permanent Secretaries to make timely decisions.
item.content:encoded:
Greater decision-making powers should be returned to Permanent Secretaries instead of being concentrated in the hands of Ministers.

USP linguistics lecturer Rajendra Prasad made the submission at the University of the South Pacific, saying the 2013 Constitution gives Ministers too much authority, limiting the ability of Permanent Secretaries to make timely decisions.

Prasad referred to chapter 4-part B Section 95 (4) of the 2013 Constitution, which allows the Prime Minister to appoint a Minister to act in the office of another Minister when that Minister is absent from duty, out of Fiji or unable to perform their duties.

He says this has resulted in more decisions being made by Ministers rather than Permanent Secretaries, who are appointed based on merit and expertise.

Prasad says before 2006, many day-to-day government decisions were made by Permanent Secretaries, allowing quicker responses to issues affecting the public.

Using flooding in Naitasiri as an example, he says Permanent Secretaries were previously able to make immediate decisions.

Insert:Prasad on decision-making 2nd July 26

He says this has slowed decision-making and believes greater authority should be returned to Permanent Secretaries so government services can respond more effectively to people's needs.

Prasad also told the Commission that the Constitution needs clearer wording to avoid different interpretations, saying too much power has been concentrated in the hands of Ministers and the Attorney-General's Office.

Meanwhile, USP researcher and lecturer Dr Sevanaia Sakai says landowners should be required to invest part of the money they receive from their natural resources to help build long-term wealth for their families.

He says the constitution should clearly define, quantify and manage the fair distribution of benefits from Fiji's mineral resources.

Sakai says the Constitution currently refers to a "fair share" of benefits but does not specify how much landowners should receive or how those benefits should be managed.

He is proposing a mandatory investment policy for income earned from natural resources, saying this would encourage long-term wealth creation instead of the money being used mainly for consumption.

Sakai says such a policy would help improve the livelihoods of iTaukei landowners and ensure resource income benefits future generations.

He also questioned why many iTaukei landowners remain poor despite owning large areas of land, saying the Constitution should help address that contradiction through better management of resource income.

item.title: Relief for Ovalau residents as Sinu-i-Wasa Tolu dismantling starts
item.description: The dismantling of the derelict passenger vessel Sinu-i-Wasa Tolu , which has been grounded at Toki on Ovalau since 2016, has begun, according to its owner, businessman Ben Naidu. Naidu made this announcement during the Fijian Media Association Levuka Town Hall Forum in Levuka , where concerns were again raised about the abandoned vessel.
item.content:encoded:
The dismantling of the derelict passenger vessel Sinu-i-Wasa Tolu , which has been grounded at Toki on Ovalau since 2016, has begun, according to its owner, businessman Ben Naidu.

Naidu made this announcement during the Fijian Media Association Levuka Town Hall Forum in Levuka , where concerns were again raised about the abandoned vessel.

Insert: Naidu - Work commenced on the Sinu-i-Wasa July 03

The announcement is expected to bring relief to residents of Toki and nearby communities, who have for years expressed concern over the deteriorating vessel.

Residents have raised fears that the ship contains hazardous materials, including diesel residue, industrial batteries and potential asbestos, posing environmental and public health risks.

The condition of the vessel has also been a longstanding concern because of its visual impact on the coastline and the potential danger it presents if left to deteriorate further.

item.title: WATCH: Minister for Finance Esrom Immanuel on fijivillage Straight Talk
item.description: Minister for Finance, Esrom Immanuel on a special fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan. The show will focus on the 2026/2027 National Budget.
item.content:encoded:
Minister for Finance, Esrom Immanuel on a special fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan.

The show will focus on the 2026/2027 National Budget.

You can watch the show on our facebook page, YouTube channel, TikTok and X account, fijivillage.

You can also catch the podcast, fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan on Spotify and Apple Podcasts.

item.title: FMA Town Hall at Tailevu Hotel from 5pm today, people in the area urged to attend
item.description: The Fijian Media Association Town Hall moves to Tailevu this afternoon as we give the opportunity to the people of Tailevu to ques

item.title: China donates $160,000 to support RFMF wives committee facility
item.description: The Embassy of the People's Republic of China in Fiji has presented a cheque of $160,000 to the Republic of Fiji Military Forces to support the construction of a dedicated facility for the RFMF Wives Committee. The cheque was presented to the RFMF Chief of Staff, Brigadier General Sapenafa Motufaga, who accepted the donation on behalf of the Force.
item.content:encoded:
The Embassy of the People's Republic of China in Fiji has presented a cheque of $160,000 to the Republic of Fiji Military Forces to support the construction of a dedicated facility for the RFMF Wives Committee.

The cheque was presented to the RFMF Chief of Staff, Brigadier General Sapenafa Motufaga, who accepted the donation on behalf of the Force.

Receiving the donation, Brigadier General Motufaga expressed his sincere appreciation to the Embassy of the People's Republic of China and the Government of China for their generous support.

He has thanked the Embassy and the Government of the People's Republic of China for this generous contribution.

He says family is at the heart of their military community, and this support will significantly enhance the welfare and development of their wives and mothers, who play an important role in supporting the servicemen and their families.

He noted that the RFMF Wives Committee has long operated without a permanent venue, with meetings and activities being held at various military units.

Brigadier General Motufaga says a dedicated facility where members can meet regularly, conduct programmes and activities, and support one another is something we have needed for some time and this generous assistance will help make that vision a reality.

He thanked the Embassy and the Government of the People's Republic of China on behalf of the RFMF and the members of the RFMF Wives Committee, describing the donation as another testament to the longstanding friendship and cooperation between Fiji and the People's Republic of China.

item.title: Assessment underway to improve Tabulotu, Dreketi community access
item.description: Assessment work is underway to improve road access for the Tabulotu community in Dreketi, Macuata, with the Ministry of Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management's Rural Outer Island Development Unit and partner agencies conducting a site visit yesterday. The assessment follows social media reports showing community members carrying an elderly resident on a makeshift wooden stretcher
item.content:encoded:
Assessment work is underway to improve road access for the Tabulotu community in Dreketi, Macuata, with the Ministry of Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management's Rural Outer Island Development Unit and partner agencies conducting a site visit yesterday.

The assessment follows social media reports showing community members carrying an elderly resident on a makeshift wooden stretcher due to poor road conditions and limited road access.

Minister for Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management, Mosese Bulitavu, directed that an urgent assessment be carried out to evaluate the situation and identify practical solutions.

Bulitavu says the Fiji Roads Authority will undertake an engineering scoping exercise in the coming days to determine the most suitable option for improving access.

He says relevant mataqali landowners have also been requested to provide the necessary approvals for land access and the sourcing of gravel required for the proposed works.

The Minister says following the technical assessment and stakeholder consultations, a project proposal will be finalised to guide the implementation of the proposed 7.5-kilometre access road improvement.

The Ministry is also working closely with the Ministry of iTaukei Affairs through the Macuata Provincial Council, which has already held consultations with the vanua to support a coordinated approach to addressing the community's accessibility challenges.

The Ministry of Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management remains committed to working with communities, landowners and partner agencies to deliver sustainable improvements to rural access infrastructure and enhance connectivity for rural communities.

item.title: Madigibuli sentenced to life for murdering pregnant wife
item.description: Apisai Madigibuli has been sentenced to life imprisonment, with a minimum term of 20 years, 9 months and 3 weeks, for killing his
